literal: appearance otherwise; presentation as other than it is
invariant_definition: *Anyathā-khyāti* is the classical Nyāya theory of erroneous cognition. In the rope-snake case the object apprehended is not sheer nothing, nor a newly fabricated entity, nor merely an undiscriminated pairing of perception and memory. Rather, a real object known elsewhere is presented here otherwise than it is, typically under the pressure of memory, defect, and a mistaken site-relation. The theory therefore presupposes the Nyāya machinery of perception, memory, defect, and practical response; error is still about the world, just not about the world correctly. Later Naiyāyikas refine the account through discussions of extraordinary perceptual relations and the exact role of remembered content in present awareness. The term matters for Vedānta because Advaita, Mīmāṃsā, Tattva-vāda (the school often labeled Dvaita), and Buddhist schools all define themselves against this realist analysis when they explain error, illusion, and finally bondage. In ordinary *vyavahāra* the theory explains why one recoils, grasps, or speaks as though a real object were present at a place where it is not.

per_school.definition: Error is apprehension of a real object under a mistaken mode or location. In rope-snake, the snake-content is not invented ex nihilo; it is grounded in a real snake previously encountered and now misrelated to the present perceptual base. Because the object is real and the defect is relational, Nyāya can preserve realism while still explaining illusion. The theory is meant to track practical behavior as well as cognition: the subject actually recoils from what is taken to be present.

Advaita rejects *anyathā-khyāti* because it thinks local mislocation of a real object cannot explain the phenomenological positivity and ontological peculiarity of illusion. The snake in rope-snake is neither simply elsewhere nor simply remembered; it is an *anirvacanīya* appearing-content generated under *avidyā*. The debate matters because the same issue scales upward: if error were only relational misplacement, the Advaitin could not use ordinary illusion as a model for the superimposed world.

Bhāṭṭa authors oppose *anyathā-khyāti* with *viparīta-khyāti*: error is a genuinely false apprehension, not merely a real object apprehended elsewhere. The point of disagreement concerns the status of the erroneous object and the amount of falsity cognition itself can bear. Bhāṭṭa Mīmāṃsā is therefore closer to Nyāya in rejecting Prābhākara *akhyāti*, but not in accepting the Naiyāyika relational analysis of the mistake.
primary_loci: Kumārila, Ślokavārttika, viparyaya section [NOT YET RETRIEVED]

per_school.definition: Mādhva authors often work with a modified realist account sometimes described as *abhinava anyathā-khyāti*. The burden of the modification is anti-Advaitin: error is still anchored in real objects and real plurality, but the Naiyāyika account is adjusted to meet later debates about presentation, memory, and the site of illusion. What never changes is the rejection of *anirvacanīya* error-content and the insistence that ordinary misperception cannot support a metaphysics of world-illusion.
primary_loci: Vyāsatīrtha, Nyāyāmṛta, khyāti section [NOT YET RETRIEVED]

translator_note: The lazy gloss is 'misperception' or 'seeing otherwise.' Those can be serviceable, but only if one keeps the Nyāya commitment in view: the error still concerns a real object in a real world. English that makes the doctrine sound like private hallucination imports the wrong metaphysics and erases the dispute with Advaita over the status of the appearing object.
